content: Portfolio rebalancing restores target asset allocations after market movements cause drift. If stocks outperform bonds, a 60/40 portfolio might become 70/30—selling stocks and buying bonds returns to target. Rebalancing enforces discipline: selling high and buying low systematically. Methods include: calendar-based (quarterly, annually), threshold-based (when allocation deviates by X%), or hybrid. Annual rebalancing captures most benefits without excessive trading costs. Tax considerations matter: rebalance in tax-advantaged accounts first, use new contributions to buy underweight assets, or employ tax-loss harvesting. Research shows rebalancing adds 0.3-0.5% annual return vs. buy-and-hold, but primary benefit is risk control—preventing portfolio from becoming too concentrated in whichever asset class has rallied.
